instruction: Rewrite the content to meet insurance regulatory and compliance standards.

input.draft_text:
Smart Investment Options for New Parents
The arrival of a child is one of the happiest moments for any family. But along with it comes the long-term financial responsibility of raising a child. As children grow, so do their needs and the costs associated with them. Education, healthcare, and future aspirations require planning well in advance. Investing wisely early on helps parents stay prepared for these rising expenses, ensuring that their child’s dreams are supported at every stage of life.
Why Should New Parents Invest Early?
Rising inflation has a direct impact on a child’s future expenses. Education inflation alone is estimated at nearly 12 %, which means the cost of education doubles in just six to seven years. Along with education, rising expenses for healthcare, skill development, groceries, and utilities steadily increase the cost of living. As a result, what you save today may not be enough to support a child’s future needs. Thus, investing becomes an essential part of any effective financial planning.
Some Popular Investment Options for New Parents
The following are some popular investment plans that new parents can consider to create a strong financial foundation for their child’s future. These investments for new parents address different goals such as education planning, long-term wealth creation, and financial security, while helping parents manage both present responsibilities and future needs.
Emergency Fund
With a new member in the family, financial responsibility increases, and unexpected expenses become harder to manage without proper planning. Maintaining an emergency fund covering at least 3-6 months of essential expenses is important to ensure financial stability even during uncertain times. Keeping this money in easily accessible liquid assets ensures quick access during situations such as job loss or medical emergencies.
PPF for Child
For parents seeking a low-risk investment for their child, the Public Provident Fund, backed by the government, offers a structured long-term solution. The account can be opened in a minor’s name by either or both parents, with a mandatory 15-year lock-in period that fits naturally into education planning timelines. Once the child becomes an adult, the account can be continued or closed based on financial needs.
Sukanya Samriddhi Yojana (For Girl Child)
Sukanya Samriddhi Yojana (SSY) is yet another savings scheme offered by the government, but this one is specially designed for the girl child. The scheme aims to encourage parents to build a long-term financial corpus for their daughter’s higher education and marriage. Launched in January 2015 as part of the Beti Bachao, Beti Padhao initiative, SSY allows parents or legal guardians of a girl child below the age of 10 to open and operate the account on her behalf. Investments are locked in for 21 years from the date of opening, with contributions required only for the first 15 years.
Contributions made for SSY qualify for deductions under Section 80C of the Income Tax Act, 1961, subject to the annual limit, while interest earned and maturity proceeds are tax-free. Parents can invest as little as ₹250 per year, with a maximum limit of ₹1.5 lakh annually, and the account can be opened at authorised banks or post offices. Once the girl child turns 18, the account operation is transferred to her, ensuring that the benefits are directed exclusively toward her future financial security.
Fixed and Recurring Deposits
Parents can invest either a lump sum through a Fixed Deposit or small, regular amounts through a Recurring Deposit, allowing savings to grow steadily until the child reaches adulthood.
FDs and RDs offer fixed returns and require no active management, which makes them suitable for parents who prefer stability over market-linked risks. They work well for short- to medium-term goals or as a supplementary savings option within a larger financial plan.
Mutual Funds and SIPs for Children
Mutual funds and SIPs offer parents a structured way to build long-term wealth for their children’s future goals, such as education or marriage. Investments can be made directly in the child’s name, with a parent or legal guardian managing the account until the child turns 18.
This approach helps create a dedicated corpus over time. You can start with a small monthly investment and give it regular boosts with amounts received as gift birthdays, and other special occasions. Starting early not only gives the advantage of compounding but also gives investment plans the time needed to short term market volatility.
Child ULIP and Insurance Plans
There are savings-oriented life insurance plans, too, that can help parents enjoy life insurance coverage and create a fund for their child’s future needs. Child insurance plans are designed specifically to create a secure corpus for the child. These types of plans usually come with a premium waiver benefit. This benefit waives the premiums payable if the parent dies during the policy term. The plan runs unaffected and offers a benefit on maturity that can help the child meet its financial needs.
ULIPs are also types of life insurance policies that combine life insurance coverage with market-linked returns. These plans can help new parents create savings for their child with market-linked returns.
National Savings Certificate (NSC)
National Savings Certificate is often preferred by parents looking for a stable and low-risk savings option for their child’s future. Backed by the Government of India, the scheme has a five-year maturity period and requires a minimum investment of ₹1,000, with no maximum deposit limit. Accounts can be opened for minors above the age of 10 by parents or guardians.
The scheme provides assured returns of 7.7% and is compounded annually. NSC certificates can also be used as collateral for loans. While it may not offer high returns, it can be used as a tool to diversify and stabilise your portfolio.
How Much Should You Invest for Your Child’s Goals?
Every parent aims to provide the best possible future for their child, but rising living standards have made education increasingly expensive. To plan effectively, parents must consider the child’s age and the years remaining before higher education begins. A longer horizon allows more flexibility in choosing investment plans and contribution amounts.
It is equally important to calculate the present cost of college education based on location and type of institution, and then account for education inflation. For example, a course costing ₹10 lakh today could cost more than ₹25 lakh after 10 years at a 12% inflation rate. Estimating the cost of goals helps you set realistic goals and helps for your children’s dreams efficiently.
Key Takeaways
Parenthood brings long-term financial responsibility, making early and structured financial planning essential to support a child’s education, healthcare, and future aspirations.
Rising inflation, especially education inflation, can significantly increase future costs, which means savings alone may not be sufficient without proper investing.
Building a financial foundation should start with an emergency fund to handle uncertainties, followed by goal-based investments aligned with the child’s future needs.
Government-backed schemes such as PPF, Sukanya Samriddhi Yojana, and NSC offer stability, discipline, and tax efficiency, making them suitable for adding stability to the investment portfolio.
Fixed and Recurring Deposits provide predictable returns for short- to medium-term needs.
Market-linked investments like mutual funds, SIPs, and child ULIP plans help create long-term wealth through market-linked returns that can compound with time.
Conclusion
Raising a child involves planning for known milestones as well as protecting against risks that cannot be predicted. Life insurance plans play a crucial role in ensuring that a child’s goals remain intact even if life takes an unexpected turn. So, assess your needs and investment strategies and choose suitable options to create funds for your child’s needs.
FAQs
What’s the safest investment for a newborn child in India?
The safest investment plans for a newborn child in India can be many, like FD, RD or government-backed schemes such as Public Provident Fund (PPF) and Sukanya Samriddhi Yojana for a girl child, as they offer capital safety, assured returns, and long-term stability.
Can both parents open a PPF or Sukanya Samriddhi account for the same child?
Only one PPF account can be opened in one name. Thus, either parent can open one PPF account in the child’s name. Similarly, only one SSY account can be opened per girl child. So, either parent can open the SSY account for the child.
How do ULIPs benefit new parents compared to simple savings plans?
ULIP plans offer dual benefits by combining life insurance coverage and the potential of long-term investment growth, ensuring a child’s financial goals remain secure while savings grow over time.
When should I start investing for my child’s education fund?
You should start investing for your child’s education fund as early as possible, ideally at birth, as a longer time horizon allows smaller investments to grow through compounding and reduces financial pressure later.
Are child investment plans tax-efficient?
Yes. Many child investment plans offer tax benefits, with contributions eligible for deductions under Section 80C and returns or maturity amounts being tax-free or tax-efficient, depending on the plan type.
Disclaimer
The content provided is for general informational purposes only. The material is compiled from publicly available sources, internal insights, and other information deemed reliable. While reasonable care has been taken in compiling the information, Bajaj Life Insurance Limited (Formerly known as Bajaj Allianz Life Insurance Company Limited) assumes no liability for its accuracy. The opinions expressed do not constitute formal recommendations, professional advice, or definitive guidance. Readers are encouraged to conduct their own due diligence and are advised to seek independent professional or expert advice before making any financial or investment decisions based on the content. Any illustrations included are for conceptual clarity only and do not reflect the actual performance of any product or offering.
Tax benefits as per prevailing Income tax laws shall apply. Please check with your tax consultant for eligibility

Smart Investment Options for New Parents
The arrival of a child is one of the happiest moments for any family. But along with it comes the long- term financial responsibility of raising a child. As children grow, so do their needs and the costs associated with them. Education, healthcare, and future aspirations require planning well in advance. Investing wisely early on helps parents stay prepared for these rising expenses, ensuring that their child’s dreams are supported at every stage of life.
Why Should New Parents Invest Early?

Along with education, rising expenses for healthcare, skill development, groceries, and utilities steadily increase the cost of living. As a result, what you save today may not be enough to support a child’s future needs. Thus, investing becomes an essential part of any effective financial planning.
Some Popular Investment Options for New Parents
The following are some popular investment plans that new parents can consider to create a strong financial foundation for their child’s future. These investments for new parents address different goals such as education planning, long-term wealth creation, and financial security, while helping parents manage both present responsibilities and future needs.
1. Emergency Fund
With a new member in the family, financial responsibility increases, and unexpected expenses become harder to manage without proper planning. Maintaining an emergency fund covering at least 3-6 months of essential expenses is important to ensure financial stability even during uncertain times1. Keeping this money in easily accessible liquid assets ensures quick access during situations such as job loss or medical emergencies1.
2. PPF for Child
For parents seeking a low-risk investment for their child, the Public Provident Fund, backed by the government, offers a structured long-term solution. The account can be opened in a minor’s name by either or both parents, with a mandatory 15-year lock-in period that fits naturally into education planning timelines. Once the child becomes an adult, the account can be continued or closed based on financial needs.
3. Sukanya Samriddhi Yojana (For Girl Child)2
Sukanya Samriddhi Yojana (SSY) is yet another savings scheme offered by the government, but this one is specially designed for the girl child. The scheme aims to encourage parents to build a long-term financial corpus for their daughter’s higher education and marriage. Launched in January 2015 as part of the Beti Bachao, Beti Padhao initiative, SSY allows parents or legal guardians of a girl child below the age of 10 to open and operate the
account on her behalf. Investments are locked in for 21 years from the date of opening, with contributions required for the first 15 years.
Contributions made for SSY qualify for deductions under Section 80C of the Income Tax Act, 1961, subject to the annual limit, while interest earned and maturity proceeds are tax-free. Parents can invest as little as ₹250 per year, with a maximum limit of ₹1.5 lakh annually, and the account can be opened at authorised banks or post offices. Once the girl child turns 18, the account operation is transferred to her, ensuring that the benefits are directed exclusively toward her future financial security.
4. Fixed and Recurring Deposits
Parents can invest either a lump sum through a Fixed Deposit or small, regular amounts through a Recurring Deposit, allowing savings to grow steadily until the child reaches adulthood.
FDs and RDs offer fixed returns and require no active management, which makes them suitable for parents who prefer stability over market-linked risks. They work well for short- to medium-term goals or as a supplementary savings option within a larger financial plan.
5. Mutual Funds and SIPs for Children
Mutual funds and SIPs offer parents a structured way to build long-term wealth for their children’s future goals, such as education or marriage. Investments can be made directly in the child’s name, with a parent or legal guardian managing the account until the child turns 18.
This approach helps create a dedicated corpus over time. You can start with a small monthly investment and give it regular boosts with amounts received as gift birthdays, and other special occasions. Starting early not only gives the advantage of compounding but also gives investment plans the time needed to short term market volatility.
6. Child based ULIP and Life Insurance Plans
There are savings-oriented life insurance plans, too, that can help parents enjoy life insurance coverage and create a corpus for their child’s future financial needs. Child based life insurance plans are designed specifically to create a secure corpus for the child. These types of plans usually come with a waiver of premium benefit. This benefit waives the premiums payable if the parent dies during the policy term. The plan runs unaffected and offers a benefit on maturity that can help the child meet its financial needs.
ULIPs are also types of life insurance plans that combine life insurance coverage with market-linked returns. These plans can help new parents create savings for their child with market-linked returns.
7. National Savings Certificate (NSC)
National Savings Certificate is often preferred by parents looking for a stable and low-risk savings option for their child’s future. Backed by the Government of India, the scheme has a
five-year maturity period and requires a minimum investment of ₹1,000 with no maximum deposit limit. Accounts can be opened for minors below 10 by parents or guardians. Minors aged more than 10 years can open their own account
The scheme provides assured returns of 7.7% (for quarter 4 of the financial year 2025- 26)and is compounded annually. NSC certificates can also be used as collateral for loans.
How Much Should You Invest for Your Child’s Goals?
Every parent aims to provide the best possible future for their child, but rising living standards have made education increasingly expensive. To plan effectively, parents must consider the child’s age and the years remaining before higher education begins. A longer horizon allows more flexibility in choosing investment plans and contribution amounts.
It is equally important to calculate the present cost of college education based on location and type of institution, and then account for education inflation. For example, a course costing ₹10 lakh today could cost more than ₹25 lakh after 10 years at a 12% inflation rate. Estimating the cost of goals helps you set realistic goals and helps for your children’s dreams efficiently.
Key Takeaways
1. Parenthood brings long-term financial responsibility, making early and structured financial
planning essential to support a child’s education, healthcare, and future aspirations. 2. Rising inflation, especially education inflation, can significantly increase future costs, which
means savings alone may not be sufficient without proper investing. 3. Building a financial foundation should start with an emergency fund to handle uncertainties,
followed by goal-based investments aligned with the child’s future needs. 4. Government-backed schemes such as PPF, Sukanya Samriddhi Yojana, and NSC offer
stability, discipline, and tax efficiency, making them suitable for adding stability to the investment portfolio. 5. Fixed and Recurring Deposits provide predictable returns for short- to medium-term needs. 6. Market-linked investments like mutual funds, SIPs, and child ULIP plans help create long-
term wealth through market-linked returns that can compound with time.
Conclusion
Raising a child involves planning for known milestones as well as protecting against risks that cannot be predicted. Life insurance plans play a crucial role in ensuring that a child’s goals remain intact even if life takes an unexpected turn. So, assess your needs and investment strategies and choose suitable options to create funds for your child’s needs.
FAQs
1. What’s the safest investment for a newborn child in India?
The safest investment plans for a newborn child in India can be many, like FD, endowment or money back life insurance plans, RD or government-backed schemes such as Public
Provident Fund (PPF) and Sukanya Samriddhi Yojana for a girl child, as they offer capital safety, assured returns, and long-term stability.
2. Can both parents open a PPF or Sukanya Samriddhi account for the same child?
Only one PPF account can be opened in one name. Thus, either parent can open one PPF account in the child’s name3. Similarly, only one SSY account can be opened per girl child. So, either parent can open the SSY account for the child4.
3. How do ULIPs benefit new parents?
ULIP plans offer dual benefits by combining life insurance coverage and the potential of wealth creation through market linked returns , ensuring a child’s financial goals remain secure.
4. When should I start investing for my child’s education fund?
You should start investing for your child’s education fund as early as possible, ideally at birth, as a longer time horizon allows smaller investments to grow through compounding and reduces financial pressure later.
5. Are child investment plans tax-efficient?
Yes. Many child investment plans offer tax benefits, with contributions eligible for deductions under Section 80C and returns or maturity amounts being tax-free or tax- efficient, depending on the plan type.
